summary:
The Boxer Protocol, signed on 7 September 1901, was the treaty that formally ended the [[event:2553|Boxer Rebellion]] and imposed the most punishing terms yet on the [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]]. Negotiated between China and the eight-nation alliance that had suppressed the rebellion, it represented a further dramatic erosion of Chinese sovereignty and independence and contributed directly to the Qing dynasty's final collapse in 1912.

The protocol's principal terms included: an indemnity of 450 million taels of silver (approximately $330 million, later funded through foreign loans that gave Western creditors leverage over Chinese revenues), to be paid over 39 years with interest, eventually totaling almost one billion taels; the execution or punishment of senior officials who had supported the Boxers; the prohibition on importing arms into China for two years; the destruction of the Taku forts guarding the approach to Tianjin; and — most consequentially — the permanent stationing of foreign troops in the Legation Quarter of Beijing and along the rail line to Tianjin.

The permanent presence of foreign military forces in Beijing symbolized the depth of China's subjugation. The massive indemnity payments drained Chinese revenue for decades. However, the United States notably chose to remit a substantial portion of its share of the indemnity and directed the funds to the Boxer Indemnity Scholarship Program, which sent thousands of Chinese students to American universities — an investment that shaped a generation of Chinese leaders and intellectuals.

The Protocol's humiliating terms intensified Chinese nationalism and contributed to the revolutionary movement that overthrew the Qing dynasty in the Xinhai Revolution of 1911–12, establishing the Republic of China.
